refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall achieve and maintain operational availability of ≥0.97 (97%) over the agreed evaluation period, measured as the ratio of time the system is available for productive operation to total scheduled operational time, accounting for planned maintenance, unplanned downtime, and safety-driven shutdowns.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-004 is a key performance indicator (KPI) that directly impacts customer satisfaction and mission success. Refining it requires explicit definition of: (1) the measurement boundary (what constitutes 'available'), (2) the evaluation period (e.g., 30 days, 90 days, 1 year), (3) exclusions (planned maintenance windows, customer-initiated pauses), and (4) the role of safety-driven shutdowns. High safety criticality demands that availability not be achieved at the expense of safety; therefore, safety-driven unavailability is acceptable and must be distinguished from failure-driven unavailability.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Collect operational telemetry (uptime/downtime logs, failure events, maintenance records) over the agreed evaluation period. Calculate availability as (Total Time − Unplanned Downtime − Safety Shutdowns) / Total Time. Compare against the 0.97 threshold. Conduct root-cause analysis on any unplanned downtime events to identify systemic failure modes.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-004.1: The NGHE solution shall detect and log all unplanned downtime events (failures, faults, anomalies) with timestamp and root cause.
FR-NGHE-004.2: The NGHE solution shall implement predictive and preventive maintenance strategies to minimize unplanned downtime.
FR-NGHE-004.3: The NGHE solution shall provide real-time availability status visibility to the Site Manager and Maintenance team.
FR-NGHE-004.4: The NGHE solution shall distinguish between safety-driven unavailability (acceptable) and failure-driven unavailability (unacceptable) in availability calculations.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement a centralized availability monitoring and analytics engine that ingests telemetry from all subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services), calculates real-time availability metrics, detects anomalies, and triggers predictive maintenance alerts.

DP-B: Distribute availability monitoring across individual subsystems (Vehicle self-monitors, Teleoperation self-monitors, Safety Services self-monitors) with periodic aggregation and reporting to a central dashboard.
DP-C: Implement a redundant architecture with hot-standby subsystems (e.g., dual Vehicle units, dual Teleoperation channels) to mask failures and maintain continuous availability.
DP-D: Implement a hybrid approach combining centralized real-time monitoring (DP-A) with distributed subsystem health checks and redundancy at critical failure points (DP-C).
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-D: Implement a hybrid availability assurance strategy combining (1) a centralized, real-time availability monitoring and analytics engine that ingests Telemetry from all subsystems, calculates availability KPIs, detects anomalies, and triggers predictive maintenance workflows; (2) distributed health-check and self-diagnostic capabilities within each subsystem (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services); and (3) strategic redundancy at critical failure points (e.g., dual communication channels, redundant safety sensors) to mask transient faults and maintain ≥0.97 availability.

potential_sources: Coupling between centralized monitoring engine and subsystem telemetry producers: mitigated by standardized Telemetry interface.
Coupling between availability analytics and maintenance scheduling: mitigated by clear ownership boundaries and asynchronous event-driven communication.
Coupling between redundancy logic and safety decision-making: mitigated by explicit SafetyAlert interface and safety-critical design reviews.
coupling_check.notes: DP-D is decoupled from specific implementation technologies (e.g., cloud vs. edge analytics, specific redundancy protocols). It defines the logical strategy without over-constraining the design domain. Subsystem independence is preserved via well-defined interfaces (Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Tasking).

assumptions_risks: Assumption: All subsystems can emit standardized Telemetry at sufficient granularity and frequency to enable real-time anomaly detection. Risk: Legacy or third-party subsystems may have limited telemetry capabilities; mitigation: adapter layers or telemetry enrichment.

Assumption: Predictive maintenance algorithms can be trained on historical failure data. Risk: Insufficient historical data in early deployment phases; mitigation: hybrid rule-based + ML approach, phased learning.

Assumption: Redundancy at critical points does not introduce unacceptable cost, weight, or complexity. Risk: Over-redundancy may violate cost or performance constraints; mitigation: FMEA-driven redundancy allocation.

Assumption: Safety-driven shutdowns are infrequent and do not dominate the availability calculation. Risk: If safety shutdowns are frequent, the 0.97 target may be unachievable without compromising safety; mitigation: separate safety availability from operational availability metrics.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Clarify the evaluation period (e.g., rolling 30-day window, quarterly, annual) and define the threshold for 'acceptable' safety-driven unavailability. Specify the minimum telemetry granularity and update frequency required from each subsystem. Conduct an FMEA to identify the top failure modes and justify redundancy allocation.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: The NGHE Solution module is the top-level system integration boundary that owns the availability KPI and orchestrates the hybrid monitoring, analytics, and redundancy strategy across all subordinate subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services). It includes the centralized availability monitoring engine, analytics pipeline, predictive maintenance workflow orchestration, and the integration logic that aggregates health signals from all subsystems and makes system-level availability decisions.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Ingests real-time operational telemetry from Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services subsystems. Includes uptime/downtime events, fault codes, sensor readings, and performance metrics. Used to calculate availability KPIs and detect anomalies.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Receives safety-critical alerts and shutdown signals from Safety Services. Used to distinguish safety-driven unavailability from failure-driven unavailability in availability calculations.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Sends maintenance task directives and operational mode adjustments (e.g., reduced-capacity mode, preventive maintenance scheduling) to Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems based on availability analytics and predictive maintenance recommendations.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Provides communication backbone for all telemetry ingestion, alert reception, and tasking dispatch. Critical for real-time availability monitoring; redundancy at this layer is essential.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the natural top-level owner of the availability KPI. It has clear ownership of the monitoring engine, analytics, and orchestration logic. Minimal diffusion: only the Telemetry ingestion and SafetyAlert reception are distributed across subsystems, but the aggregation and decision logic is centralized. This is the correct architectural choice.

Vehicle could own its own availability monitoring (self-diagnostics, health checks), but the system-level availability KPI and cross-subsystem analytics must reside at NGHE Solution level. Assigning the full DP to Vehicle would fragment the availability strategy and create diffusion across multiple subsystems.

Similar to Vehicle: Teleoperation can own its own health monitoring, but the system-level availability orchestration and predictive maintenance workflows must be centralized. Assigning to Teleoperation would create asymmetric ownership and complicate cross-subsystem redundancy decisions.

Safety Services owns the SafetyAlert interface and safety-driven shutdown logic, but not the overall availability KPI. Assigning the full DP to Safety Services would conflate safety availability with operational availability, violating separation of concerns.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The NGHE Solution module is the correct owner of the availability KPI and the hybrid monitoring/analytics/redundancy strategy. It has the highest containment score (0.95) and the lowest diffusion score (0.05). The strategy is logically cohesive: centralized monitoring and analytics, distributed subsystem health checks, and strategic redundancy all serve the single goal of achieving ≥0.97 availability. Ownership is clear, and interfaces to subordinate subsystems are well-defined (Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Tasking, Network Link).
containment_risks: Risk: The NGHE Solution module may become a bottleneck if the centralized monitoring engine is not sufficiently scalable or resilient. Mitigation: Design the monitoring engine with high availability (e.g., distributed processing, failover), and ensure it does not introduce a single point of failure.

Risk: Telemetry ingestion from multiple subsystems may create a high-bandwidth, low-latency requirement on the Network Link. Mitigation: Implement telemetry filtering, aggregation, and prioritization to reduce bandwidth; use edge processing where feasible.

Risk: Predictive maintenance algorithms may require significant computational resources and historical data. Mitigation: Phased rollout with rule-based heuristics initially, ML models added as data accumulates.
Risk: Redundancy at critical points may introduce complexity and cost. Mitigation: FMEA-driven allocation; focus redundancy on the highest-impact failure modes.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Define the internal architecture of the NGHE Solution module: (1) specify the monitoring engine (centralized vs. distributed processing), (2) define the telemetry schema and update frequency, (3) specify the predictive maintenance algorithm(s) and training data requirements, (4) define the redundancy strategy (which subsystems/components are redundant, how failover is triggered), and (5) specify the Network Link redundancy and failover logic. Conduct a detailed design review to ensure the monitoring engine itself does not become a bottleneck or single point of failure.

fr_dp_gate.reason: The refined FR clearly states the availability target (≥0.97), measurement boundary (operational time), and evaluation period. DP-D directly addresses this by implementing a hybrid strategy combining centralized monitoring, distributed health checks, and strategic redundancy. The DP is logically sound and decoupled from specific implementation technologies. The mapping is one-to-one and non-redundant: the DP fully satisfies the FR without over-specification.

diffusion_risks: Telemetry ingestion is distributed across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services, but aggregation and analytics are centralized in NGHE Solution. This is acceptable and follows the principle of separation of concerns.

Redundancy logic may be distributed across subsystems (e.g., Vehicle has dual sensors, Teleoperation has dual channels), but the orchestration of redundancy failover is centralized in NGHE Solution. This is acceptable and ensures consistent availability decisions.
Predictive maintenance workflows may involve tasking to multiple subsystems, but the decision logic is centralized. This is acceptable and ensures coordinated maintenance scheduling.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: The NGHE Solution module has high containment (0.95) and low diffusion (0.05). The DP is well-aligned with the IM boundary. The external interfaces (Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Tasking, Network Link) are all defined and available. No new interfaces need to be invented. The ownership is clear: NGHE Solution owns the availability KPI and the orchestration logic; subsystems own their own health checks and contribute telemetry.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification is feasible via operational telemetry collection and analysis. The refined FR specifies a measurable KPI (≥0.97 availability) and a clear evaluation period. Verification requires: (1) instrumentation of all subsystems to emit Telemetry, (2) collection and aggregation of uptime/downtime logs, (3) root-cause analysis of failures, and (4) comparison against the 0.97 threshold. This is a standard operational verification approach.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: The allocation is clear: NGHE Solution owns the availability KPI and the monitoring/analytics/redundancy strategy. Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services own their own health checks and contribute telemetry. The interfaces are well-defined. Ownership boundaries are unambiguous.

recommended_revisions: Clarify the evaluation period (rolling window, quarterly, annual) and define the acceptable threshold for safety-driven unavailability.
Specify the minimum telemetry granularity and update frequency required from each subsystem.
Conduct an FMEA to identify the top failure modes and justify redundancy allocation.
Define the internal architecture of the NGHE Solution module: monitoring engine design, telemetry schema, predictive maintenance algorithms, and redundancy strategy.
Specify the Network Link redundancy and failover logic to ensure the monitoring engine itself is highly available.

decision.justification: The refined FR is clear and measurable, the selected DP is logically sound and decoupled, and the assigned IM (NGHE Solution) has high containment and clear ownership. All gates pass. However, the DP is not atomic: it comprises three distinct strategies (centralized monitoring, distributed health checks, strategic redundancy) that require further decomposition to guide detailed design. The sub-FRs identified in the refined_fr section (FR-NGHE-004.1 through FR-NGHE-004.4) are the natural entry points for the next decomposition level. Proceeding to the next level will clarify the design and enable detailed implementation planning.
decision.justification_detail: This is a KPI-level requirement that inherently requires decomposition into lower-level functional and design requirements. The hybrid DP strategy is sound but requires further refinement at the next level to address: (1) telemetry collection and anomaly detection (FR-NGHE-004.1), (2) predictive maintenance workflows (FR-NGHE-004.2), (3) availability visibility and reporting (FR-NGHE-004.3), and (4) safety vs. failure distinction (FR-NGHE-004.4). Proceeding to the next level is the correct path.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-004.1: The NGHE solution shall detect and log all unplanned downtime events (failures, faults, anomalies) with timestamp and root cause.
FR-NGHE-004.2: The NGHE solution shall implement predictive and preventive maintenance strategies to minimize unplanned downtime.
FR-NGHE-004.3: The NGHE solution shall provide real-time availability status visibility to the Site Manager and Maintenance team.
FR-NGHE-004.4: The NGHE solution shall distinguish between safety-driven unavailability (acceptable) and failure-driven unavailability (unacceptable) in availability calculations.

assumptions: All subsystems can emit standardized Telemetry at sufficient granularity and frequency.
Predictive maintenance algorithms can be trained on historical failure data or rule-based heuristics.
Redundancy at critical points is cost-effective and does not violate other constraints.
Safety-driven shutdowns are infrequent and do not dominate the availability calculation.
The evaluation period is defined by the customer (e.g., 30 days, 90 days, 1 year).
The 0.97 availability target is achievable without compromising safety or other performance requirements.
open_risks: Telemetry ingestion may create high bandwidth and low-latency requirements on the Network Link.
The centralized monitoring engine may become a bottleneck or single point of failure if not designed with high availability.
Predictive maintenance algorithms may require significant computational resources and historical data.
Redundancy allocation may be over-specified or under-specified if not driven by FMEA.
Safety-driven unavailability may be frequent, making the 0.97 target unachievable without compromising safety.
Subsystem health checks may not be sufficiently granular to enable accurate root-cause analysis.
evidence_needed: Historical failure data from similar NGHE deployments or comparable equipment.
Telemetry schema and update frequency specifications from Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services subsystems.
FMEA results identifying the top failure modes and their impact on availability.
Redundancy strategy and failover logic specifications.
Network Link capacity and latency specifications.
Predictive maintenance algorithm specifications and training data requirements.
Operational verification plan and acceptance criteria for the 0.97 availability target.
